## Discussion

This study demonstrates that the primary barrier to efficient, reproducible systematic reviews is not the difficulty of individual steps but the fragmentation between them. meta-pipe — a 10-stage, quality-gated pipeline — reduced the production time of a pairwise meta-analysis from an estimated 100+ hours to 14 hours while achieving GRADE HIGH certainty, 27/27 PRISMA compliance, and full computational reproducibility. The methodological contribution is not the automation per se but the integration: connecting stages with enforced quality transitions that prevent the silent error propagation, retroactive patching, and reproducibility gaps that characterize manual workflows.

### Pipeline Thinking versus Tool Thinking

The current landscape of AI tools for evidence synthesis reflects what might be called "tool thinking" — building the best possible solution for screening, or extraction, or risk-of-bias assessment, each as an isolated problem. This mirrors an earlier phase in other data-intensive biomedical disciplines. In genomics, individual alignment and variant-calling tools existed for years before pipeline frameworks like nf-core [@ewels2020] standardized the connections between them, transforming reproducibility from an aspiration into an enforceable property. In neuroimaging, the Brain Imaging Data Structure (BIDS) [@gorgolewski2016] achieved similar gains not by improving individual analysis steps but by standardizing the data handoffs between them. Evidence synthesis is arriving late to this pipeline paradigm — and the cost of fragmentation has been substantial. Published audits consistently find that a significant proportion of systematic reviews contain numerical errors, many traceable to manual data transfer between extraction and analysis [@jones2005; @buscemi2006]. These are not errors of method but errors of process — exactly the kind that pipeline integration is designed to prevent.

### Standardization as a Methodological Dividend

An underappreciated benefit of pipeline integration is that it enables cross-project comparison of process metrics. When each systematic review follows an ad hoc workflow, it is impossible to benchmark: how long should screening take for a review of this scope? What is a normal kappa for a cardiology versus oncology topic? What proportion of reviews achieve PRISMA compliance on first pass? meta-pipe's standardized stage definitions and quality gates produce structured metadata — time per stage, quality gate pass/fail rates, overclaim detection counts — that, across a sufficient number of projects, could establish evidence-based norms for evidence synthesis itself. This is a form of meta-research that the current fragmented workflow makes impractical.

### Living Systematic Reviews Become Feasible

The concept of living systematic reviews — periodically updated to incorporate new evidence — has been endorsed by Cochrane and others as essential for rapidly evolving clinical questions [@elliott2017]. In practice, living reviews remain rare because each update cycle repeats much of the manual labor of the original review. Pipeline automation fundamentally changes this calculus. When the entire workflow from search to manuscript is encoded as an executable pipeline, an update becomes a re-execution with modified search dates rather than a reconstruction from scratch. The quality gates ensure that updates meet the same methodological standards as the original, and the artifact versioning system preserves the complete history of changes across iterations. meta-pipe does not yet implement automatic scheduled re-execution, but the architecture is designed to support it — each stage's inputs and outputs are fully specified, and the orchestration layer can detect which downstream stages need re-execution when upstream data changes.

### Human-in-the-Loop Design

A critical design decision in meta-pipe is where to retain human oversight and where to allow autonomous execution. The five decision points — PICO confirmation, analysis type selection, screening conflicts, risk-of-bias judgment, and manuscript interpretation — were selected because they require either clinical domain expertise (PICO, RoB, interpretation), irreversible methodological commitments (analysis type), or subjective judgment that cannot yet be reliably validated automatically (screening conflicts). This is a deliberate contrast with fully autonomous approaches: we argue that the value of pipeline automation is not to remove humans from evidence synthesis but to ensure that human effort is concentrated where it has the highest marginal value, while mechanical tasks (search execution, deduplication, format conversion, figure generation, compliance checking) are handled reliably and reproducibly by the pipeline.

### Limitations

Several limitations warrant discussion. First, we demonstrate meta-pipe on a single case study — a pairwise meta-analysis of 5 RCTs in a well-defined clinical area. The pipeline's performance on larger reviews, reviews with high heterogeneity, mixed study designs, or network meta-analyses remains to be validated. Second, the pipeline currently depends on Claude (Anthropic) as the LLM backbone; performance and cost implications of alternative models have not been evaluated. Third, the 14-hour completion time includes only the time of a single experienced operator familiar with the pipeline — the learning curve for new users has not been assessed. Fourth, the search was limited to PubMed and Scopus; while this meets the minimum PRISMA requirement of two databases, some reviews may require additional sources (Embase, Cochrane CENTRAL, grey literature). Fifth, all demonstration work was conducted in English; the pipeline's handling of multilingual literature has not been tested.

### Future Directions

The immediate next step is a formal validation study: reproducing 5–10 published Cochrane reviews using meta-pipe and comparing screening sensitivity/specificity, extraction accuracy, effect estimate concordance, and production time against the published results. This would follow the approach of otto-SR, which reproduced 12 Cochrane reviews, but extend the comparison to include the analysis and manuscript stages that otto-SR does not cover. Longer-term directions include multi-LLM support (to reduce single-vendor dependency), automated living review scheduling, and development of evidence-based benchmarks for evidence synthesis process metrics.

### Conclusions

The fragmentation of the systematic review workflow into disconnected manual steps is not merely an efficiency problem but a methodological one, introducing errors at data handoffs, preventing retroactive quality enforcement, and limiting reproducibility to the statistical layer. meta-pipe demonstrates that pipeline integration — connecting stages with quality gates, standardized data formats, and human-in-the-loop decision points — can address these problems while reducing production time by 87–93%. The pipeline paradigm, long established in genomics and neuroimaging, offers a path toward more reliable, reproducible, and sustainable evidence synthesis.
